problem_summary: 2017 MCM/ICM Problem E (sustainability / "smart growth"). The team had to: (1) build a metric to evaluate how successful a city's "smart growth" plan is under the "three E's" (Economic prosperity, social Equity, Environmental sustainability); (2) apply it to two mid-sized cities on different continents — they chose Wellington, NZ (~450k) and Anchorage, Alaska, USA (~300k) — and judge how well each city's current plan meets the 10 smart-growth principles; (3) design a new growth plan per city; (4) rank the plan's individual initiatives by potential and compare the two cities; (5) stress-test the plan under a 50% population increase by 2050. Their solution: a 3-tier weighted index (SSG = 0.3 SEP + 0.3 SSE + 0.4 SES) with 9 first-class and 23 second-class indicators, scored via Grey Relational Analysis against benchmark US smart-growth cities, with PCA to pick principal indicators, GM(1,1) grey forecasting to project trends, and a Malthusian model for the population-growth scenario.
models_used: Hierarchical weighted-index metric system: SSG = 0.3·SEP + 0.3·SSE + 0.4·SES, with 3 first-class + multiple second-class indicators per pillar (23 total)

Grey Relational Analysis (GRA) — normalize indicators (higher/lower/middle-is-better), pick a reference series from benchmark cities (Atlanta, Portland, Austin), compute grey relational coefficient γ_ij = (Δmin + τΔmax)/(Δij + τΔmax) with resolution ratio τ=0.5, then average to a relational grade per indicator
Principal Component Analysis (PCA) via SPSS — orthogonal transform to pick the principal (principle) indicators driving each pillar, used to focus which initiatives to design
Grey Forecasting Model GM(1,1) — project each indicator from 8 observations (2007–2014) into short(5y)/medium(10y)/long(30y) term under current vs proposed plan
Malthusian (exponential) population-growth model dP/dt = rP, fitted r≈1.16%/yr, to model the +50% population-by-2050 scenario and re-adjust plan assumptions
Shannon diversity index — used to quantify business diversity, occupational diversity, religious freedom and racial equity indicators (H = -Σ d_i ln d_i)
Galster et al. 'Wrestling Sprawl' sprawl-dimension formulas — concentration, mixed-use, clustering metrics for the land-use (SES2) sub-indicators
Index of potential p_i = (S_pi − S_oi)/S_oi — percentage change in an indicator's relational grade with vs without the plan, used to rank initiatives
key_techniques: Mapping the qualitative problem frame (3 E's goals × 10 principles) into an explicit goal-to-tool table (Table 1) by keyword-extracting from the EPA / Smart Growth America source documents — turning a policy narrative into a structured G1/G2/G3 mapping

Benchmark-as-reference-series trick: instead of an abstract ideal, GRA's reference series is the empirical average of cities that have done smart growth for a long time (Atlanta/Portland/Austin), making 'success' concrete and data-grounded
Defining a 4-band interpretation scale (high smart growth / smart growth / sprawl / high sprawl) with deliberately overlapping ranges to translate raw relational grades into a verdict

Two-stage indicator hierarchy (first-class → second-class) so weighting and PCA happen at the level where data exists, and missing data (e.g. policy participation, water pollution) can be dropped cleanly

Scenario-driven assumption setting: each initiative is tied to specific indicators with explicit short/medium/long-term percentage uplifts, so the same evaluation pipeline can re-score 'current plan' vs 'our plan'

Closing the loop — the same SSG+GRA scoring engine is reused for evaluation (Task 2), plan comparison (Task 3), initiative ranking (Task 4), and the population stress test (Task 5), giving a unified, self-consistent methodology
Sensitivity analysis on the GRA resolution ratio τ (0.07→1.0) to justify the τ=0.5 choice and show the with-plan grade stays above without-plan across the range
why_it_won: From a judge's view this paper wins on coverage, coherence, and traceability rather than on any single sophisticated model. (1) It answers every sub-task explicitly and visibly, and crucially it reuses ONE evaluation engine (SSG + GRA) across all five tasks, so the paper reads as a single coherent system instead of five disconnected mini-models. (2) Every indicator and component is sourced to a real authority (EPA case studies, Smart Growth America, Statistics NZ, AreaVibes/City-Data) — judges reward modeling choices that are justified rather than invented. (3) The benchmark-city reference series makes 'success' empirical and defensible. (4) It layers complementary, recognizable techniques (GRA for evaluation, PCA for dimension reduction, GM(1,1) for forecasting, Malthus for population) — breadth signals modeling maturity. (5) It is honest: it openly drops un-quantifiable indicators, lists data-deviation/subjectivity/few-reference-cities weaknesses, and runs a sensitivity check. (6) Strong scaffolding — summary sheet, assumptions-with-justifications, variable table, radar/3D/bar visualizations, and an appendix flowchart — makes it skimmable and self-validating for a fast-reading judge.
section_highlights: The Summary Sheet is the standout: in one page it names every method (SSG metric, GRA, PCA, GM(1,1), Malthus) and states results, so a triage judge instantly sees the full arc. Section 4 (Policy Tools, Table 1) is a model of turning a 10-principle policy narrative into a clean G1/G2/G3 intention mapping. Section 5 builds the SSG metric pillar-by-pillar (SEP/SSE/SES) each with a Venn-diagram decomposition into three components and a tidy indicator table with ↑/↓ targets — very readable. Section 6.2 lays out GRA as numbered steps with the exact normalization and γ_ij formulas, then gives a relational-grade table + radar chart + a 4-band verdict scale. Section 7 is the strongest structurally: it follows EPA's explicit 3-step plan-formulation recipe (set goals → identify strengths/barriers → select tools/initiatives), produces per-city initiative tables (W1–W7, A1–A7) each tied to specific principles and indicators, then re-scores them. Section 7.5's index-of-potential ranking and 7.7's resolution-ratio sensitivity plot close the analytic loop. Weaknesses are candidly enumerated in 8.2.
pitfalls_or_limitations: Heavy subjectivity baked into the core: the SSG weights (0.3/0.3/0.4) are asserted, not derived (no AHP/entropy weighting despite citing AHP in refs), and the team itself admits many indicators are 'born of our own experience and intuition'

Forecasting on extremely thin data: GM(1,1) is fitted on only 8 yearly observations (2007–2014) and then extrapolated 30+ years to 2050 — grey forecasting is fragile and this is well beyond a defensible horizon

The 'our plan vs current plan' improvement is essentially assumption-driven: the percentage uplifts (5%/10%/20% etc.) per initiative are hand-set 'in accordance with reference cities', so the result that 'our plan performs better' is partly engineered by the assumptions rather than independently validated

Notation/consistency slips that hurt rigor: SES_21 formula is printed twice for two different concepts (concentration vs clustering), the SSE_31 and SSE_32 Shannon formulas are written identically, and headers mislabel a section 'Metric of SEP' twice

Data quality is shaky and acknowledged: indicators pulled from heterogeneous websites (AreaVibes, City-Data, Stats NZ) with different statistical standards; some indicators (policy participation = Meaningful Involvement, water pollution) were dropped entirely for lack of data, leaving SSE2 unquantified
Only 3 benchmark reference cities, all US — using US cities as the 'ideal' to score Wellington, NZ is a cross-context fairness problem the team flags but doesn't resolve
Malthusian (pure exponential) population model is the crudest possible choice (no logistic/carrying-capacity), and the +50% scenario mostly just rescales the same hand-set assumptions
reusable_patterns: The 'one evaluation engine, reused across all tasks' pattern: build a single composite index + scoring method early, then reuse it for evaluation, comparison, ranking, and scenario stress-tests — yields a coherent paper and saves modeling effort
Two-tier indicator hierarchy (first-class pillars → second-class measurable indicators) with explicit ↑/↓ targets, so weighting/PCA operate where data exists and missing indicators drop out cleanly

Benchmark-as-reference-series: for any 'how good is X' question, source an empirical 'gold standard' set (here, mature smart-growth cities) and measure distance to it via GRA — far more defensible than an abstract ideal

GRA recipe for multi-criteria evaluation with messy/uncertain data: classify indicators (higher/lower/middle-is-better) → normalize → reference series → grey coefficient with τ=0.5 → average to grade → sensitivity-check τ
Keyword-extraction from authoritative source docs to build a justified goal→tool mapping table — converts a qualitative policy/standard into a structured, citable model component

Index-of-potential ranking: define improvement as percentage change in a metric with-vs-without intervention to rank competing initiatives/policies — a clean, general way to answer 'which action matters most'

Tie every proposed initiative to specific indicators with explicit short/medium/long-term uplift assumptions, then re-run the scoring engine — makes plan evaluation reproducible and the assumptions auditable
Layer complementary standard models for breadth: evaluation (GRA) + dimension reduction (PCA) + forecasting (GM(1,1)) + domain dynamics (Malthus) — a reusable MCM C/E-paper toolkit combination

Scaffolding template that reads well to judges: summary sheet naming all methods+results, assumptions-with-justifications, variable table, per-component Venn/indicator tables, radar/bar visualizations, candid strengths/weaknesses, sensitivity plot, appendix flowchart
